coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of hollow carbon fiber/carbon quantum dots composite aerogels for oil sorption
coredata.dc:creator: Ibarra Torres, Cynthia E.
Serrano Quezada, Thelma E.
Kharissova, Oxana V.
Zeng, Hongbo
Kharisov, Boris I.
Luevano Hipólito, Edith
Torres-Martínez, Leticia M.
González, Lucy T.
coredata.dc:description: Oil spill accidents are presented as a severe environmental problem because they cause ecological damage in the short and long term, especially in the aquatic environment. For this reason, the development of efficient materials to separate oil/water mixtures is fundamental. Studies have been performed using aerogels to fulfill this purpose because they are materials with a high porosity and high surface area, formed by solid particle networks and interconnected pores; these properties make them ideal for application as petroleum adsorbents. In this study, a simpler synthesis process using only renewable and environmentally friendly reagents for obtaining hollow carbon fiber/carbon quantum dots composite aerogels (CF-QD) is presented. Sorbent materials were prepared using an easy dip-coating technique and pyrolysis. Analyzing the effect of the concentration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in aerogels on the properties and adsorption capacity. CF-QD manifested remarkable characteristics, including a high surface area (up to 360.7 m2/g), hydrophobic surface, excellent buoyancy, and good adsorption capacity (up to 84.1 g/g). These features make them a viable option for application as sorbents for organic liquids, including oil.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of hollow carbon fiber/carbon quantum dots composite aerogels for oil sorption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oil spill accidents are presented as a severe environmental problem because they cause ecological damage in the short and long term, especially in the aquatic environment. For this reason, the development of efficient materials to separate oil/water mixtures is fundamental. Studies have been performed using aerogels to fulfill this purpose because they are materials with a high porosity and high surface area, formed by solid particle networks and interconnected pores; these properties make them ideal for application as petroleum adsorbents. In this study, a simpler synthesis process using only renewable and environmentally friendly reagents for obtaining hollow carbon fiber/carbon quantum dots composite aerogels (CF-QD) is presented. Sorbent materials were prepared using an easy dip-coating technique and pyrolysis. Analyzing the effect of the concentration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in aerogels on the properties and adsorption capacity. CF-QD manifested remarkable characteristics, including a high surface area (up to 360.7 m2/g), hydrophobic surface, excellent buoyancy, and good adsorption capacity (up to 84.1 g/g). These features make them a viable option for application as sorbents for organic liquids, including oil.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Crude oil is considered an essential energy resource because it is used to produce gasoline, other fuels, and chemical products. It is also an essential resource for economic development and stability worldwide (Cepni et al., 2022; Karasu and Altan, 2022; Manowska and Bluszcz, 2022; Wu, 2023). Because of the intense demand for energy, the exploitation and transportation of oil have been promoted. Incidents that cannot be expected either in time or place may arise, causing oil spills (Kang et al., 2023a; Chang, 2021; Jin et al., 2022).

Oil spills cause environmental damage and severe mental and physical health problems for people near the spill, including oil spill cleanup workers and visitors. This has short- and long-term consequences and affects the economy, society, and culture. As a result, remediating an oil spill becomes a matter of great importance (Khoi et al., 2023; Sandifer et al., 2021; Afenyo et al., 2022; Ogbuka et al., 2022; Seara et al., 2022). It is important to mention that oil spills turn water into wastewater (Panagopoulos, 2021, 2022a; Panagopoulos and Giannika, 2022a). The discharge of wastewater can cause serious environmental problems, waste of water resources, and degradation of water quality; so it cannot be used directly for drinking water (through desalination) or industrial applications (Panagopoulos and Giannika, 2022b; Panagopoulos, 2022b; Deng et al., 2021).

Aerogels have been presented as an excellent alternative for oil spill remediation because of their excellent textural properties, adjustable surface chemistry, hydrophobicity, very low density, extremely high surface area per unit volume, good hydrocarbon sorption capacity, good reusability, and some of them are biodegradable (Setyawan et al., 2022; Mariana et al., 2022; Ahmed et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023a).

Carbon materials have the advantages of being nontoxic, lightweight, and have high thermal, mechanical, and chemical stability (Lin et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022a; Zhang et al., 2022). In particular, carbon aerogels have been considered ideal candidates for the adsorption cleanup of oil spills or other organic liquids because their open porous network facilitates the penetration of molecules from the outside to the inside of the material (Ma et al., 2022; Zhai et al., 2021; Cui et al., 2022). Various precursors have been used to produce carbon aerogels. For example, biomass such as sugarcane bagasse and coffee grounds can be used to obtain hybrid carbon aerogels (adsorption capacities up to 10.023 g/g) (Phat et al., 2022a), metal-organic frameworks, carbon nanotubes and cotton to obtain composite carbon aerogels (adsorption capacities of 48–84 g/g) (Sun et al., 2022b), poplar catkin to get fiber-based aerogel with tubular-lamellar interweaved neurons-like structure (adsorption capacities of 28.8–78.1 g/g) (Dong et al., 2022), waste-peanut shells to obtain a carbon fiber aerogel (adsorption capacities of 27–50 g/g) (Dai et al., 2022), coir to synthesize carbon aerogels (motor oil sorption capacity of 22.71 g/g) (Nguyen et al., 2022), shaddock peel (adsorption capacities of 23–48 g/g) (Liu et al., 2022) or pomelo peel (adsorption capacities up to 11.672 g/g) (Phat et al., 2022b) to produce carbon aerogels. These materials achieved excellent adsorption capacities by making use of renewable raw materials, and even discarded, which represents a great advantage for the production of these adsorbents.

Graphene aerogels (adsorption capacities of 42–396 g/g) (Bo et al., 2021; Saleem et al., 2022; Nassar et al., 2022; Diao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2022), carbon nanotube (CNT) aerogels (adsorption capacities up to 107 g/g) (Khoshnevis et al., 2018; Arum et al., 2021), hollow carbon spheres/graphene aerogels (adsorption capacities of 58–108 g/g) (Hou et al., 2019), magnetic carbon nanospheres/graphene composite aerogel (adsorption capacities of 187–537 g/g) (Kang et al., 2020), graphene/CNT hybrid aerogels (adsorption capacities 21–501 g/g) (Huang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2017a; Shen et al., 2017; Zhan et al., 2018; Farbod and Madadi Jaberi, 2021; Luo et al., 2020), N-doped carbon fibers/reduced graphene oxide aerogels (adsorption capacities up to 206.38 g/g) (Qin et al., 2021), and graphene/carbon nanofibers/carbon nanotubes aerogels (adsorption capacities up to 273 g/g for hot engine oil) (Zhang et al., 2021), have also been obtained. In hybrid or composite aerogels, the individual properties of each component are synergistically combined to generate a material with improved properties (Qin et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022; Sonu et al., 2023). For example, the nanoroughness of graphene sheets is increased as a result of the presence of CNTs. The hydrophobicity of the aerogel containing these two carbon allotropes is increased.

To contribute to solving environmental problems caused by the leakage of crude oil or organic liquids into the water, the aim of this work is to develop carbon aerogels, specifically hollow carbon fibers/CQDs aerogels, for their application as adsorbents for oil in spills. Cotton has been used to obtain carbon aerogels with very good results. This precursor has the characteristics of being inexpensive and renewable, two great advantages for obtaining oil adsorbents. Although cotton has been investigated to obtain oil sorbents (Li et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2022c; Wang and Liu, 2019; Cheng et al., 2017; Shin et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2015, 2017b), this work pursues to avoid the use of potentially hazardous reagents for the environment or humans, expensive reagents, or complicated processes. CQDs were selected to modify the nanoroughness of the cotton fibers because their precursor is innocuous and the production method is simple and scalable (Jia et al., 2012), also CQDs have not been widely investigated in oil sorbent materials.

For CQDs synthesis, ascorbic acid (SIGMA, ≥99%) was used as a carbon source using the solution thermolysis technique. For this, 0.37 g of ascorbic acid were dissolved in 40 mL of water. The mixture was stirred for 10 min at room temperature. Then, the resulting product was maintained at 90 °C under stirring for 5.5 h, and then it was cooled to room temperature to get the CQDs solution.

The cotton (purchased from a local store) was impregnated with the CQDs solution at two different concentrations (75 and 50 %V/V of the CQDs solution diluted with distilled water) for 24 h. The cotton fibers were then dried at 40 °C for 14 h, and the fibers impregnated with the CQDs were carbonized at 700 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere to obtain the corresponding aerogels (called CF-QD75 and CF-QD50, according to the concentration of the CQDs solution). The hollow fiber aerogel (CF) was obtained without the CQDs for comparison. Fig. 1 shows the synthesis process of CQDs and CF-QD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption tests and contact angle measurement
ce:section.ce:para: Adsorption tests were carried out according to what is indicated in the ASTM F726-99 standard (Li et al., 2013; Hoang and Pham, 2021). In a 100 mL beaker, 50 mL of crude oil (Texas oil) or ethanol (DEQ, >99.6%) was poured. Then, the aerogel was weighed and immersed in the oil for 10 min. Afterward, it was removed from the beaker, left for 30 s to drain the excess oil and weighed.

Adsorption capacity, Q, is reported as the amount of oil adsorbed, in grams, for each gram of adsorbent, according to Eq. (1). The weight of the oil adsorbed is calculated by the mass difference, subtracting the weight of the dry adsorbent from the weight of the aerogel after adsorption.
(1)
Q =
w e i g h t
o f
a d s o r b e d
o i l
d r y
a d s o r b e n t
w e i g h t
Adsorption tests were performed three times for each material.

Static contact angles of CF-QD were measured by applying 5 μL of deionized water at room temperature on the surface of aerogels. A high-resolution camera captured the droplet images, and the contact angles were the average of three measurements of a water droplet at different positions on each sample. In this study, the contact angles were measured using ImageJ software.

CQDs synthesis by the thermolysis of ascorbic acid presents the stages of polymerization and carbonization. During heating, polymerization occurs, forming aromatic compounds and oligosaccharides. The high concentration of ascorbic acid results in nucleation. This carbonization step may arise from crosslinking induced by the intermolecular dehydration of linear or branched oligosaccharides, or other macromolecules. Then, the nuclei grow until the final size is reached.

CQDs were analyzed by FTIR. Fig. 2 a) shows the spectrum obtained, where is observed a broad band centered at 3389 cm−1 characteristics of the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. At 2935 cm−1 the stretch vibration bands of the C–H sp
2 and sp
3 bonds are observed. At 1712 cm−1 the band corresponding to the stretching vibration of the CO bond is observed. At 1634 cm−1 the band corresponding to the stretching vibration of the aromatic CC bonds is found. Between 1157 and 1034 cm−1 the vibration bands that are because of the stretching of the C–O bond are appreciated. The band at 777 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration C–H aromatics.

Photoluminescence spectroscopy analyzes were performed on this material. In the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, shown in Fig. 2 b), a single emission band is observed in the spectrum. This is a characteristic behavior of CQDs, obtaining a maximum emission at 462 nm, which suggests that CQDs emit in blue.
Fig. 2 c) shows the XRD result for CQDs, where the peak corresponds to the (002) plane, showing the obtention of CQDs with disordered graphitic structure.

CF and CF-QD materials were formed according to the aforementioned methodology. Because of their lightweight, the obtained materials can stand on green bristle grass. This type of materials present buoyancy in water because of their low density (Fig. 3 ).

As it can be seen in the SEM images, Fig. 4 , the micrographs of the aerogels show that in these materials there is a porous structure made up of a three-dimensional and interconnected network composed of randomly oriented hollow carbon fibers. Fig. 4 d) and g) show the three-dimensional structure obtained for CF-QD50 and CF-QD75. It can be observed for CF-QD50 that the material is formed by a network of perfectly formed hollow fibers. For CF-QD75, it can be observed that some fibers did not preserve their morphology and broke, producing a type of corrugated sheets. For these materials, it can be observed that the CQDs were dispersed throughout the surface of the fibers. Fig. 4 e) and f) show a good dispersion of the CQDs on the CF-QD50 fibers with the formation of small clusters. Fig. 4 h) and i) show that large agglomerations of CQDs were present for the CF-QD75 sample; increase in the concentration of CQDs in the solution used during the synthesis of this material caused this. In summary, CF-QD50 featured perfectly formed hollow carbon fibers, with small clusters of CQDs, but a better distribution of them was noted throughout the surface of the fibers.

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental analyzes demonstrated a uniform distribution of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) elements in the CF-QD50, as shown in Fig. 4 j). A typical EDS spectrum for this type of material was obtained. Where the elemental distribution indicates that the predominant element in the material is carbon. The EDS results for CF-QD75 can be found in Fig. 4 k), where a uniform distribution of C can be observed. This does not occur with O, which presents agglomerations in specific areas.

The obtained CF-QD were analyzed by FTIR, and the spectra are shown in Fig. 5 A). For CF, the band corresponding to the stretching vibration of the CC bonds at 1576 cm−1 can be observed. For CF-QD, the absence of bands corresponding to the groups can be observed, which indicates carbonization of the cotton fibers. The functional groups that were present in the CQDs were removed.

Fig. 5 B) presents the XRD analyzes of the aerogels. For the CF, it can be seen that three diffraction peaks were obtained. The weak peak present at 10.5° in 2θ could be attributed to the plane (1
1 ‾
0) corresponding to the crystalline structure of cellulose (Yuan et al., 2018). The most intense peak between 23 and 36° corresponds to the plane (002), and the peak present between 37 and 44° corresponds to the plane (001). These two peaks are characteristic of amorphous carbon. For all CF-QD, the peak (1
1 ‾
0) disappears and the diffraction signals corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes are still present, situated between 18-30° and 41–49°. This shows that the crystalline structure of cellulose was destroyed and only the amorphous carbon product of pyrolysis was present.

Using the nitrogen physisorption technique, the adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained for the CF and CF-QD, which are shown in Fig. 6 a) and c) for CF and CF-QD50 samples. Type IV isotherms, which are characteristic of mesoporous materials, can be observed. Using the BJH method, the pore size distributions were obtained, which are shown in Fig. 6 b) and d), observing that these materials are predominantly mesoporous.

Table 1 shows the results obtained for the BET surface area, main pore radius, and total pore volume for CF-QD50 and CF. Noting that the surface area increases considerably by adding the CQDs for the CF-QD50 sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wettability and oil adsorption of CF-QD
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophobicity of aerogels is a property of great importance for their application as oil adsorbents. It is an indicator of the selectivity that the materials present toward organic liquids and not toward water (Iskandar et al., 2022; Duman et al., 2022). The contact angle of water on a substrate is a good indicator of hydrophobicity.

The contact angle results are shown in Fig. 7 a). For the CF-QD, it is observed that, compared with pristine CF, the contact angle increases when adding the CQDs for the CF-QD50 sample because the CQDs are distributed across the surface of the hollow fibers, increasing their nanoroughness. The contact angle decreases with the increasing quantity of CQDs in the CF-QD75 sample. This may be because there is not an appropriate distribution of CQDs on the surface of the fibers because the CQDs agglomerate, leaving areas on the surface of the fibers that do not have CQDs.

The adsorption capacity test results for the CF-QD are presented in Fig. 7 b). A notable increase in the adsorption capacity can be observed when adding the CQDs in the CF-QD50 sample compared with the CF. This may be due to the homogeneous distribution of the CQDs on the fibers. CQDs modify the surface morphology and wettability of the fibers; producing an increase in nanoroughness of the material and provides a contact angle of 121°, this is a hydrophobic material. The presence of CQDs increases the surface area of the material. This aerogel has the largest surface area of this group of materials with 360.786 m2/g, which is a high surface area.

When the quantity of CQDs present in the aerogel for the CF-QD75 sample is increased, a decrease in the adsorption capacity is observed. This could be because there is not a good distribution of the CQDs on the surface of the hollow fibers, presenting CQDs clusters and leaving areas on the surface of the fibers that do not present CQDs, for which a decrease in the contact angle is also noted.

Table 2 shows a comparison of the results obtained in this study and other similar works. This table shows the adsorption capacity considering the type of oil for a more appropriate comparison. CF-QD50 featured the highest oil and ethanol adsorption capacity. The only work that presents a simpler methodology than that of this work was to obtain carbon fiber aerogels (Bi et al., 2013). CF-QD50 obtained a higher adsorption capacity for ethanol. This work does not study the adsorption capacity of crude oil. As previously mentioned, CQDs have not been extensively studied in oil adsorption applications. Finding their use only for obtaining polyurethane sponge embedded with a PDMS layer and carbon dots (Singh and Jelinek, 2020) and carbon dots as high-density hot spots in melamine sponge (Su et al., 2022). The adsorption capacity of CF-QD50 was greater than that obtained in these works.

CF-QD50 obtained a higher sorption capacity than other recent works, for example, other biomass-based materials such as a core–shell sorbent using coir fibers covered with a thin layer of shell made from cotton (5.7–6.5 g/g) (Raju et al., 2022), Fe3O4 biomass-based activated carbon nanocomposite (6.84–9.60 g/g, 8.72 g/g for light crude oil) (Hammouda et al., 2022), magnetic corncob powder (13.33–57.65 g/g, 22.13 g/g for crude oil) (Rezaei and Hassanajili, 2023), modified corn stalk pith (46.43 g/g) (Peng et al., 2023), vertically aligned-biomass fiber junctioned sorbents with CNT, polyvinyl alcohol, and methyltrimethoxysilane (50 g/g for xylene) (Kang et al., 2023b); aerogels such as polyimide-graphene nanoplatelet aerogel composites (7.3 g/g for diesel) (Tafreshi et al., 2023), an aerogel using nanocellulose and natural rubber latex (30–67 g/g) (Chhajed et al., 2023), silica aerogel with electrospun SiO2 nanofibers (10.3 g/g for CH2Cl2) (Liu et al., 2023b), bamboo-shaped oxidized multi-walled CNT-silica aerogel (19.48 g/g for motor oil) (AlZubi et al., 2023), layered nanofiber/sheet aerogels by 2D electrospinning polycaprolactone nanofiber (25.60–42.13 g/g) (Dong et al., 2023), waste plastic-derived aerogel modified with graphene oxide and PDMS (27 g/g) (Tu et al., 2023); or other sorbents like a water-repellent MIL-101(Fe) material supported over fluorinated graphene nanosheets (4.4–5.6 g/g, 2.3 g/g for oil) (Ravi et al., 2022), bio-based polyurethane foam (65 g/g for diesel) (Olivito et al., 2023). The crude oil sorption capacity of CF-QD50 is similar to other sorbents such as polyurethane sponges modified by multi-walled carbon nanotubes, hexagonal boron nitride@Fe3O4, nano graphite, and acrylic resin (83.5 g/g) (Habibi et al., 2023), nanofibrous sorbents from 4,4′-(hexafluoroisopropylidene)diphthalic anhydride (6FDA)-based polyimides (83 g/g) (Topuz et al., 2022), or carbon fiber aerogels produced from lignocellulosic biomass (81.6 g/g) (Aytekin et al., 2023).

A significant factor in the application of a material as an adsorbent for oil and/or organic liquids is its reusability. This is its ability to be used on several occasions before being discarded. The best adsorption capacity was obtained for CF-QD50, so reuse tests were conducted using this material. The results of the reuse tests using oil and ethanol as adsorbates are presented in Fig. 7 c). Tests were carried out with ethanol to compare the performance of the aerogel as an adsorbent of organic liquids of low density and viscosity. When using this material to adsorb ethanol, stable behavior is noted after the second adsorption-combustion cycle. Maintaining 77.5% of the initial adsorption capacity in cycle three and 71.2% in cycle ten. When adsorbing crude oil, a very notable decrease in the adsorption capacity can be observed. Having lost practically half of its adsorption capacity for the third cycle of use (it maintains 52.5% of the initial adsorption capacity) and for cycle number seven, its adsorption capacity is practically completely lost (it maintains 1.4% of the initial adsorption capacity).

For this material, a notable difference can be perceived in its recyclability when it is used as an adsorbent for ethanol and oil. This is because of the notable differences between these organic liquids. The oil is more viscous, dense, less volatile, and it can present a solid residual phase that is difficult to remove from the adsorbent. At the beginning of the 10th cycle, the weight of the adsorbent before adsorption, i.e., after eliminating the oil adsorbed from the previous cycle, was 196% greater than the initial weight of the adsorbent. This can be attributed to the fact that the adsorbed oil could not be completely eliminated from the adsorbent. Retaining a fraction of the adsorbed oil would form a film that would decrease the surface area and pore size of the material, resulting in a lower adsorption capacity.

Mesoporous materials comprising a three-dimensional and interconnected network composed of hollow carbon fibers and CQDs were developed. The highest adsorption capacity was obtained by the CF-QD50, which presented an adsorption capacity of 84.1 g/g, 29.78% higher than that of the pristine CF. This is because in this material, a uniform distribution of the CQDs was achieved on the surface of the hollow fibers. This results in a material with a high surface area of 360.786 m2/g, the highest obtained by these materials, and a contact angle of 121°. The improved adsorption capacity obtained for the CF-QD50 aerogel compared with the pristine CF aerogel is partly due to the improved nanoroughness of the fiber surface. Other characteristics obtained, such as the increase in the surface area, pore volume, and hydrophobicity, are also relevant for improving the adsorption capacity. This material presents good recyclability when used to adsorb ethanol, maintaining 71.2% of the initial adsorption capacity in cycle ten. Recyclability is lower for oil adsorption, where almost half of its adsorption capacity is lost for the third cycle of use (52.5% of the initial adsorption capacity is maintained). This is due to the accumulation of oil in the material and the formation of a film. This decreases the surface area and pore size, making it difficult for the adsorbate to pass into the adsorbent. This material is a viable option for use as an oil adsorbent in spills because of its adsorption capacity and recyclability. Because of its high thermal stability, the adsorbed oil can be removed by combustion or distillation to be reused. This work provides an opportunity to integrate CQDs into petroleum sorbent materials as a new field of study.
All authors worked on the conception, experimental design, measurements, writing, editing, and revision of the manuscript.
